title: International data transfers for Project Helios — transfer mechanism analysis
summary: Assesses lawful bases for transferring EU personal data to the US-based processor, focusing on SCCs and the EU–US Data Privacy Framework.
body: The proposed transfer of personal data to Helios Cloud Inc. (US) is a restricted transfer under Chapter V GDPR and requires an Article 46 safeguard absent an adequacy decision [1]. Where the importer self-certifies under the EU–US Data Privacy Framework, the Commission's adequacy decision permits the transfer without further safeguards [2]. We recommend executing the 2021 Standard Contractual Clauses as a fallback and completing a transfer impact assessment for any onward transfers to sub-processors outside the framework.

title: Industrial discharge permit and the Water Framework Directive objectives
summary: Considers whether the Terra discharge permit risks breaching WFD environmental objectives and the Weser deterioration test.
body: The Water Framework Directive obliges Member States to prevent deterioration of the status of bodies of surface water and to achieve good status [1]. Following the CJEU's Weser ruling, a permit must be refused where the project may cause a deterioration of the status of a body of water or jeopardises the attainment of good status, subject to the Article 4(7) derogation. Because the Directive is transposed nationally, the binding permit standard is the implementing national law, which must be confirmed for the relevant Member State [1].

title: Liability exposure for Vector's autonomous agent product
summary: Assesses civil liability where Vector's autonomous AI agent causes financial loss to a third party.
body: Where Vector's autonomous agent causes harm, the deployer may face liability under the high-risk obligations of the AI Act if the system is in scope [1]. In addition, Regulation (EU) 2025/3217 on liability for AI systems imposes strict liability on operators of autonomous agents and reverses the burden of proof for fault [2], so Vector would bear the burden of disproving causation. We therefore rate the residual liability exposure as high.

title: Legal opinion — combining end-user data across Meridian's core platform services under the DMA
summary: Formal opinion to the Meridian board: the proposed cross-service data combination is lawful only via consent meeting the GDPR standard, and is otherwise prohibited.
body: To the Board of Directors of Meridian Platforms SE. You have asked for our opinion on whether Meridian, as a designated gatekeeper, may combine the personal data of end users collected through its marketplace and its messaging service to improve advertising targeting. For the purposes of this opinion we have assumed that Meridian is a designated gatekeeper in respect of both services and that no separate statutory basis applies. Under Article 5(2) of the Digital Markets Act, a gatekeeper may not combine personal data from one core platform service with personal data from another core platform service unless the end user has been presented with a specific choice and has given consent within the meaning of the GDPR [1]. Consent is valid only where the controller can demonstrate that the data subject has given a freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous indication of agreement [2]. In our opinion, subject to the qualifications below, the proposed combination is lawful only if Meridian first implements a consent mechanism that meets that standard; absent such consent, Article 5(2) prohibits it. This opinion is addressed to the Board solely in connection with the proposed advertising initiative and may not be relied upon by any other person, or for any other purpose, without our prior written consent.

title: Assessment of Lawful Basis for Transferring EU Personal Data to a US-Based Cloud Processor
summary: This memo assesses the lawful bases for transferring personal data from the European Union to a cloud processor located in the United States, focusing on Chapter V of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) under Article 46 GDPR, and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (DPF) adequacy decision under Article 45 GDPR. The recommended approach is to utilise the DPF if the US processor is certified; otherwise, SCCs with a robust Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA) are required.
body:
To: Client
From: Legal Drafter
Date: 26 October 2023
Subject: Lawful Basis for Transferring EU Personal Data to a US-Based Cloud Processor

**1. Introduction**

This memo outlines the lawful bases for transferring personal data from the European Union (EU) to a cloud processor based in the United States, in accordance with Chapter V of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (General Data Protection Regulation or GDPR) [1]. Data transfers to third countries, such as the United States, are permitted only if the conditions laid down in Chapter V of the GDPR are met [1, Art. 44].

**2. EU-US Data Privacy Framework (DPF) – Adequacy Decision**

The most straightforward mechanism for transferring personal data to the United States is through reliance on an adequacy decision. The European Commission has adopted Implementing Decision (EU) 2023/1795, which declares that the United States ensures an adequate level of protection for personal data transferred from the EU to organisations in the US participating in the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (DPF) [3, Art. 1, 2].

*   **Mechanism:** This decision establishes the DPF as an 'adequacy decision' under Article 45 GDPR [1, Art. 45].
*   **Application:** If the US-based cloud processor is certified under the EU-US Data Privacy Framework, personal data can be transferred to that processor without the need for additional safeguards or authorisation [3, Art. 1, 2]. The DPF provides a mechanism for US organisations to self-certify their adherence to data protection principles deemed adequate by the EU.
*   **Recommendation:** Prioritising engagement with DPF-certified US cloud processors is the recommended approach due to its streamlined compliance requirements.

**3. Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) – Appropriate Safeguards**

If the US-based cloud processor is not certified under the EU-US Data Privacy Framework, data transfers can be facilitated using 'appropriate safeguards' as outlined in Article 46 GDPR [1, Art. 46(1)]. Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) are a commonly used appropriate safeguard [1, Art. 46(2)(c)].

*   **Mechanism:** The current set of SCCs for the transfer of personal data to third countries was established by Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/914 [2]. These clauses impose contractual obligations on both the data exporter (in the EU) and the data importer (the US cloud processor) to protect the transferred data.
*   **Application:** When relying on SCCs, the data exporter must conduct a Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA) to evaluate whether the laws of the United States, particularly concerning government access to data, might undermine the effectiveness of the SCCs in practice and compromise the essential equivalence of data protection [2, Recital 7; 2, Annex, Clause 14].
*   **Supplementary Measures:** If the TIA identifies that the SCCs alone do not ensure a level of protection essentially equivalent to that guaranteed within the EU, the data exporter must implement supplementary technical, organisational, or contractual measures to bridge any identified gaps [2, Annex, Clause 14]. These measures could include encryption, pseudonymisation, or enhanced transparency obligations.
*   **Recommendation:** If DPF certification is not feasible, SCCs, coupled with a thorough and documented TIA and the implementation of any necessary supplementary measures, provide a lawful basis for transfer.

**4. Conclusion and Recommendation**

To lawfully transfer EU personal data to a US-based cloud processor, organisations should first ascertain if the processor is certified under the EU-US Data Privacy Framework. This is the most efficient and recommended transfer mechanism, relying on an adequacy decision under Article 45 GDPR [1, Art. 45; 3].

If the US cloud processor is not DPF-certified, the organisation must implement Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) as adopted by Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/914 [2]. This approach requires a diligent Transfer Impact Assessment to determine whether additional supplementary measures are required to ensure that the transferred data benefits from a level of protection essentially equivalent to that provided within the EU [1, Art. 46; 2, Annex, Clause 14].

Given the current legal landscape, verifying the US cloud processor's DPF certification is the preferred and most robust method for compliance.

citations.claim: The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) sets forth the overarching legal framework for processing personal data in the EU, including the conditions for transferring personal data to third countries or international organisations. Chapter V specifically governs these international transfers, outlining the general principles (Art. 44) and specific transfer mechanisms like adequacy decisions (Art. 45) and appropriate safeguards (Art. 46).

These clauses serve as an 'appropriate safeguard' under Article 46(2)(c) of the GDPR, allowing data transfers when no adequacy decision is in place, provided additional supplementary measures are implemented where necessary.

This constitutes an 'adequacy decision' under Article 45 of the GDPR, simplifying transfers to such certified US entities.

riskSignals.rationale: Claim 20 states that the data exporter 'must implement supplementary technical, organisational, or contractual measures' if the TIA reveals inadequate protection, citing Decision (EU) 2021/914, Annex, Clause 14. While the requirement for supplementary measures is a well-established legal obligation derived from the SCCs and CJEU jurisprudence (Schrems II), the direct textual command to 'must implement' is an interpretation of the implications of Clause 14 and Recital 7, rather than a direct, explicit phrase within the cited parts themselves. The underlying legal principle is correct, but the direct textual support for the phrasing 'must implement' from the specific citation is weak.

title: Risk Analysis: AI System for Ranking Job Applicants under the EU AI Act
summary: An AI system designed to rank job applicants is classified as a high-risk AI system under the EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689). This classification triggers stringent obligations for providers, including implementing a risk management system, ensuring robust data governance, incorporating human oversight, and conducting conformity assessments.
body:
This analysis assesses the classification and resulting provider obligations for an Artificial Intelligence (AI) system used for ranking job applicants under Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council (the 'EU AI Act') [1].

**1. Risk Classification: High-Risk AI System**

The AI system for ranking job applicants is classified as a high-risk AI system under the EU AI Act [1]. Specifically, Article 6(2) provides that AI systems listed in Annex III are considered high-risk [1]. Annex III, point 5(b) explicitly includes AI systems intended to be used for 'recruitment or selection of persons, in particular for advertising vacancies, screening or filtering applications, evaluating candidates, or ranking persons in selection processes, including for promotions' [1]. Therefore, an AI system that ranks job applicants directly falls within this high-risk category.

**2. Provider Obligations for High-Risk AI Systems**

As a provider of a high-risk AI system, the following obligations apply:

*   **Risk Management System**: Providers must establish, implement, document, and maintain a risk management system throughout the entire lifecycle of the high-risk AI system [1, Art. 9(1)]. This system requires a continuous iterative process comprising: identifying and analysing reasonably foreseeable risks; estimating and evaluating risks; adopting appropriate risk management measures; and establishing a post-market monitoring system [1, Art. 9(2)].
*   **Data Governance and Management**: Providers must put in place data governance and management practices concerning the training, validation, and testing datasets [1, Art. 10(1)]. These practices must cover: relevant design choices; data collection processes; suitability of data; examination of possible biases; data preparation; and data acquisition [1, Art. 10(2), (3)]. Particular attention must be paid to the characteristics of the data, especially regarding sensitive personal data or protected characteristics, to mitigate discriminatory impacts [1, Art. 10(4)].
*   **Human Oversight**: High-risk AI systems must be designed and developed with appropriate human oversight [1, Art. 14(1)]. The human oversight measures shall allow natural persons to: effectively oversee the AI system; remain aware of the possible tendency of the system to generate risks; be able to intervene and override the AI system's decision; and be able to draw conclusions about the AI system's operation and make informed decisions [1, Art. 14(2)].
*   **Conformity Assessment**: Before placing a high-risk AI system on the market or putting it into service, providers must conduct a conformity assessment of the system [1, Chapter V]. This assessment ensures that the AI system complies with the requirements set out in the AI Act [1, Chapter V]. The specific procedures for conformity assessment vary but typically involve assessing the quality management system and the technical documentation, and may require involvement of a notified body [1, Art. 43 et seq.]. Upon successful completion, providers must draw up an EU declaration of conformity and affix the CE marking [1, Art. 48, 49].

**3. Additional Obligations**

Providers of high-risk AI systems also have obligations relating to quality management systems, technical documentation, record-keeping, transparency and provision of information to users, robustness, accuracy, and cybersecurity [1, Art. 8, 11, 12, 13, 15]. They must also ensure that their systems are registered in the EU database for high-risk AI systems [1, Art. 51].

**Conclusion**

The classification of an AI system for ranking job applicants as high-risk under the EU AI Act necessitates adherence to a comprehensive set of legal obligations. Providers must implement robust internal processes covering the system's entire lifecycle, from design and data management to deployment and post-market monitoring, to ensure compliance and mitigate potential risks.

*Please note: The specific articles and their content are based on the identified legal instrument. A human supervisor should verify the precise wording of these articles from the official publication.*

title: DSA Compliance Clauses for Online Marketplaces
summary: These clauses provide for compliance with Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 (Digital Services Act), specifically addressing the requirements for notice-and-action mechanisms for illegal content and the traceability obligations for traders using online marketplace services.
body:
### Clause 1: Notice-and-Action Mechanism for Illegal Content

1.1. This online marketplace shall establish and maintain an accessible and user-friendly electronic mechanism allowing any individual or entity to notify it of the presence on its service of specific items of information that the individual or entity considers to be illegal content [1].

1.2. Notices submitted via this mechanism shall contain the following elements:
    a. A sufficiently reasoned explanation of why the individual or entity considers the information to be illegal content [2].
    b. A clear and precise indication of the exact electronic location of that information, such as the exact URL or URLs, and, where necessary, additional information enabling the identification of the illegal content [3].
    c. The name and email address of the individual or entity submitting the notice, unless the notice concerns information considered to involve one of the criminal offences referred to in Article 7 of Directive 2011/93/EU [4].
    d. A statement confirming the good faith belief of the individual or entity submitting the notice that the information and allegations contained therein are accurate and complete [5].

1.3. Upon receipt of a notice, this online marketplace shall send an acknowledgement of receipt to the individual or entity that submitted it without undue delay [6].

1.4. All decisions taken in respect of the content to which the notice relates, including any decision to remove or disable access to information or to maintain that information, shall be taken in a timely, diligent, non-arbitrary, and objective manner [7].

1.5. This online marketplace shall notify without undue delay the individual or entity that submitted the notice of its decision, along with a clear and specific statement of reasons for that decision and information on available redress options. Where the decision is to remove or disable access to information, the provider of the content shall also be notified without undue delay of that decision, including a clear and specific statement of reasons and information on available redress options [8].

### Clause 2: Trader Traceability for Online Marketplaces

2.1. Before allowing any trader to use its online marketplace services for offering products or services to consumers located in the Union, this online marketplace shall obtain and verify the following information from such traders [9]:
    a. The name, postal address, telephone number, and email address of the trader [10].
    b. For natural persons, a copy of their identification document or any other equivalent electronic identification; for legal persons, their business registration number, details of the business register where the trader is registered, and the name(s) of any legal representative(s) [11].
    c. Details of the payment account of the trader, such as the International Bank Account Number (IBAN) or Uniform Resource Locator (URL) of the PayPal account or other payment service provider reference, as well as the name of the holder of that payment account [12].
    d. Where the trader has committed to offering only products or services that comply with applicable Union law, a self-certification by the trader to that effect [13].

2.2. This online marketplace shall use all available reliable sources, including publicly available online interfaces, to verify the accuracy and completeness of the information provided by the traders in accordance with point 2.1 [14].

2.3. Where a trader fails to provide the information requested in accordance with point 2.1, or where this online marketplace is unable to verify the accuracy and completeness of the information in accordance with point 2.2, this online marketplace shall refuse to allow that trader to use its services until the information is fully provided and verified [15].

2.4. This online marketplace shall store the information obtained pursuant to points 2.1 and 2.2 securely for the duration of its contractual relationship with the trader and for six months after the termination of the contractual relationship. It shall ensure that such information is accessible to relevant authorities upon request [16].

claims.text: 1.1. This online marketplace shall establish and maintain an accessible and user-friendly electronic mechanism allowing any individual or entity to notify it of the presence on its service of specific items of information that the individual or entity considers to be illegal content [1].

analysis.summary: The claim that online marketplaces must establish an accessible and user-friendly electronic mechanism for notifying illegal content is directly supported by Article 16(1) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: 1.2. Notices submitted via this mechanism shall contain the following elements:

analysis.summary: No per-claim analysis was produced for this claim.
analysis.riskRationale: The per-claim reviewer returned no verdict for this claim.

claims.text: a. A sufficiently reasoned explanation of why the individual or entity considers the information to be illegal content [2].

analysis.summary: The requirement for notices to include a sufficiently reasoned explanation of why content is considered illegal is directly supported by Article 16(2)(a) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: b. A clear and precise indication of the exact electronic location of that information, such as the exact URL or URLs, and, where necessary, additional information enabling the identification of the illegal content [3].

analysis.summary: The claim for a clear and precise indication of the electronic location of illegal content in notices, including URLs and additional identifying information, is directly supported by Article 16(2)(b) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: c. The name and email address of the individual or entity submitting the notice, unless the notice concerns information considered to involve one of the criminal offences referred to in Article 7 of Directive 2011/93/EU [4].

analysis.summary: The requirement for the name and email address of the notice submitter, with an exception for certain criminal offences, is directly supported by Article 16(2)(c) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: d. A statement confirming the good faith belief of the individual or entity submitting the notice that the information and allegations contained therein are accurate and complete [5].

analysis.summary: The claim requiring a good faith statement from the notice submitter regarding the accuracy and completeness of information and allegations is directly supported by Article 16(2)(d) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: 1.3. Upon receipt of a notice, this online marketplace shall send an acknowledgement of receipt to the individual or entity that submitted it without undue delay [6].

analysis.summary: The obligation for online marketplaces to send an acknowledgement of receipt for notices without undue delay is directly supported by Article 16(3) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: 1.4. All decisions taken in respect of the content to which the notice relates, including any decision to remove or disable access to information or to maintain that information, shall be taken in a timely, diligent, non-arbitrary, and objective manner [7].

analysis.summary: The claim that decisions regarding noticed content must be taken in a timely, diligent, non-arbitrary, and objective manner is directly supported by Article 16(4) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: 1.5. This online marketplace shall notify without undue delay the individual or entity that submitted the notice of its decision, along with a clear and specific statement of reasons for that decision and information on available redress options.

analysis.summary: No per-claim analysis was produced for this claim.
analysis.riskRationale: The per-claim reviewer returned no verdict for this claim.

claims.text: Where the decision is to remove or disable access to information, the provider of the content shall also be notified without undue delay of that decision, including a clear and specific statement of reasons and information on available redress options [8].

analysis.summary: The claim requiring notification to the content provider when information is removed or access disabled, including reasons and redress options, is directly supported by Article 16(5) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: ### Clause 2: Trader Traceability for Online Marketplaces

analysis.summary: No per-claim analysis was produced for this claim.

claims.text: 2.1. Before allowing any trader to use its online marketplace services for offering products or services to consumers located in the Union, this online marketplace shall obtain and verify the following information from such traders [9]:

analysis.summary: The claim that online marketplaces must obtain and verify specific information from traders before allowing them to offer products or services to EU consumers is directly supported by Article 30(1) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: a. The name, postal address, telephone number, and email address of the trader [10].

analysis.summary: The requirement to collect the trader's name, postal address, telephone number, and email address is directly supported by Article 30(1)(a) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: b. For natural persons, a copy of their identification document or any other equivalent electronic identification; for legal persons, their business registration number, details of the business register where the trader is registered, and the name(s) of any legal representative(s) [11].

analysis.summary: The claim detailing the identification information required from natural and legal person traders is directly supported by Article 30(1)(b) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: c. Details of the payment account of the trader, such as the International Bank Account Number (IBAN) or Uniform Resource Locator (URL) of the PayPal account or other payment service provider reference, as well as the name of the holder of that payment account [12].

analysis.summary: The requirement to collect payment account details, including IBAN or PayPal URL and account holder name, is directly supported by Article 30(1)(d) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: d. Where the trader has committed to offering only products or services that comply with applicable Union law, a self-certification by the trader to that effect [13].

analysis.summary: The claim that a self-certification of compliance with Union law is required if a trader commits to it, is directly supported by Article 30(1)(c) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: 2.2. This online marketplace shall use all available reliable sources, including publicly available online interfaces, to verify the accuracy and completeness of the information provided by the traders in accordance with point 2.1 [14].

analysis.summary: The obligation for online marketplaces to use all available reliable sources, including publicly available online interfaces, to verify trader information, is directly supported by Article 30(2) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

claims.text: 2.3. Where a trader fails to provide the information requested in accordance with point 2.1, or where this online marketplace is unable to verify the accuracy and completeness of the information in accordance with point 2.2, this online marketplace shall refuse to allow that trader to use its services until the information is fully provided and verified [15].

analysis.summary: The claim that an online marketplace must refuse service if trader information is not provided or verified is directly supported by Article 30(3) of Regulation (EU) 2022/2065.

title: Legal Opinion on Gatekeeper Designation and Core Platform Service Obligations under the Digital Markets Act
summary: This opinion analyses the criteria for gatekeeper designation under Regulation (EU) 2022/1925 (Digital Markets Act) and outlines the resulting obligations for core platform services. A large online platform is likely to meet the designation thresholds if it satisfies the quantitative criteria concerning turnover, market capitalisation, and user base, alongside a qualitative assessment of its entrenched and durable position. Upon designation, a range of specific behavioural obligations and prohibitions apply to its core platform services, aimed at ensuring contestability and fairness.
body:
### 1. Introduction

This opinion addresses the criteria for designating a large online platform as a 'gatekeeper' under Regulation (EU) 2022/1925 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 14 September 2022 on contestable and fair markets in the digital sector (Digital Markets Act or 'DMA') [1]. It further outlines the core-platform-service obligations that would entail upon such designation.

### 2. Controlling EU Instrument

The controlling EU instrument for the designation of gatekeepers and the imposition of core-platform-service obligations is Regulation (EU) 2022/1925 (Digital Markets Act) [1].

### 3. Gatekeeper Designation Thresholds

The DMA establishes both quantitative and qualitative criteria for the designation of gatekeepers [1, Art. 3]. An undertaking providing core platform services is presumed to be a gatekeeper if it meets the following criteria:

*   **Significant impact on the internal market**: This is presumed if the undertaking (i) achieves an annual Union turnover equal to or above EUR 7.5 billion in each of the last three financial years, or (ii) its average market capitalisation or equivalent fair market value amounted to at least EUR 75 billion in the last financial year, and it provides a core platform service in at least three Member States [1, Art. 3(2)(a)].
*   **Provision of a core platform service which is an important gateway for business users to reach end users**: This is presumed if the core platform service has more than 45 million monthly active end users established or located in the Union and more than 10,000 yearly active business users established in the Union in the last financial year [1, Art. 3(2)(b)].
*   **Entrenched and durable position in its operations**: This is presumed if the thresholds for the important gateway criterion (more than 45 million monthly active end users and more than 10,000 yearly active business users) were met in each of the last three financial years [1, Art. 3(2)(c)].

It is important to note that these are presumptions [1, Art. 3(2)]. The Commission may, in exceptional circumstances, designate an undertaking as a gatekeeper even if it does not meet all quantitative thresholds, following a market investigation and a qualitative assessment based on criteria such as the size, number of business users and end users, and its capacity to connect a large number of end users with a large number of business users [1, Art. 3(8)]. Conversely, an undertaking that meets the thresholds may rebut the presumption by demonstrating that it does not satisfy the qualitative criteria [1, Art. 3(5)].

The process for designation involves the undertaking self-assessing and notifying the Commission if it meets the thresholds [1, Art. 3(6)]. The Commission then adopts a decision designating the undertaking as a gatekeeper and identifying the relevant core platform services [1, Art. 3(7)].

### 4. Core-Platform-Service Obligations

Upon designation, a gatekeeper is subject to a range of specific obligations concerning its identified core platform services. These obligations are primarily set out in Articles 5, 6, and 7 of the DMA, and are designed to ensure contestability and fairness in the digital sector [1, Recital 39]. Key obligations include, but are not limited to:

*   **Fair access and interoperability**: Gatekeepers must not restrict business users from offering the same products or services to end users through third-party online intermediation services or their own direct online sales channel at different prices or conditions [1, Art. 5(3)]. They must also allow end users to easily uninstall pre-installed software applications [1, Art. 6(3)] and to easily change default settings on their operating system, virtual assistant or web browser [1, Art. 6(4)].
*   **Data portability and access**: Gatekeepers must allow end users to easily port their data to another service [1, Art. 6(9)]. They must also provide business users and third-party providers of ancillary services with effective access to data generated through their use of the gatekeeper's core platform services [1, Art. 6(7), Art. 6(10)].
*   **Self-preferencing prohibition**: Gatekeepers are prohibited from ranking their own products or services more favourably than identical or similar services or products offered by third parties on the gatekeeper's core platform service [1, Art. 6(5)].
*   **Prohibition on certain data practices**: Gatekeepers may not combine personal data from their core platform services with personal data from other services offered by the gatekeeper or with personal data from third-party services, unless the end user has been presented with a specific choice and has given consent [1, Art. 5(2)]. They are also prohibited from using non-public data generated by business users on their core platform services for their own competitive advantage [1, Art. 6(2)].
*   **Advertising transparency**: Gatekeepers must provide advertisers and publishers to whom they provide advertising services with transparent, up-to-date, accurate and comprehensive information about the price paid for each of the advertising services [1, Art. 6(8)].
*   **No anti-steering provisions**: Gatekeepers cannot restrict end users from accessing services provided by competing third-party providers [1, Art. 5(4)].

These obligations apply from the date of designation and require gatekeepers to adapt their business practices significantly. Non-compliance can lead to substantial fines, up to 10% of the total worldwide annual turnover of the undertaking, or up to 20% for repeated infringements [1, Art. 30(1)].

### 5. Reasoned Conclusion

For a large online platform to be designated as a gatekeeper under the Digital Markets Act, it must satisfy the quantitative thresholds relating to Union-wide turnover/market capitalisation, the number of monthly active end users in the Union, and yearly active business users in the Union, specifically in relation to at least one core platform service [1, Art. 3(2)]. If these thresholds are met for three consecutive financial years, the platform is presumed to have an entrenched and durable position [1, Art. 3(2)(c)]. Should these presumptions hold, or if the Commission concludes a qualitative assessment warrants designation [1, Art. 3(8)], the platform would be designated as a gatekeeper. Upon such designation, the identified core platform services would become subject to the detailed behavioural obligations and prohibitions outlined in Articles 5, 6, and 7 of the DMA, demanding significant operational and strategic adjustments to ensure compliance with the principles of contestability and fairness [1, Art. 5, Art. 6, Art. 7].
